question: Define a compact metric space and state the Heine-Borel theorem for the real line.
expectedPoints: A metric space (X, d) is compact if every open cover of X has a finite subcover.
The Heine-Borel theorem states that a subset of R^n is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
Specifically for R^1, a subset of the real numbers is compact precisely when it is closed and bounded.

question: Prove that a closed subset of a compact metric space is compact.
expectedPoints: Let (X, d) be a compact metric space and let F be a closed subset of X.
Consider an arbitrary open cover {U_α} of F.
Since F is closed, its complement F^c is open. Therefore, the collection {U_α} ∪ {F^c} is an open cover of X.
By the compactness of X, this open cover has a finite subcover. This finite subcover must cover all of X, and hence all of F.
If the finite subcover includes F^c, we can remove it, as it is not needed to cover F. The remaining finite collection of U_α sets is a finite subcover of the original cover for F.

question: Explain the relationship between compactness, sequential compactness, and total boundedness (completeness) in a metric space. Prove one direction of the equivalence that is central to this relationship.
expectedPoints: In a metric space, the following are equivalent: (i) compactness, (ii) sequential compactness (every sequence has a convergent subsequence), and (iii) completeness and total boundedness.
Focus on proving that sequential compactness implies total boundedness.
Assume, for contradiction, that a sequentially compact metric space (X, d) is not totally bounded.
This means there exists an ε > 0 such that X cannot be covered by a finite number of ε-balls.
Construct a sequence (x_n) inductively: choose x_1 arbitrarily. Then choose x_2 such that d(x_2, x_1) >= ε. Then choose x_3 such that d(x_3, x_1) >= ε and d(x_3, x_2) >= ε. Continue this process.

This construction yields a sequence where the distance between any two distinct terms is at least ε. Such a sequence cannot have a convergent (and hence Cauchy) subsequence, contradicting sequential compactness.
